Qs.Headline & Outlet Metadata:
Fill with verifiable facts only:
• Headline text
• Publication/outlet name
• Publication date (YYYY‑MM‑DD or "Unknown")
• Author name(s) exactly as credited
• Outlet section label (e.g., Opinion, Editorial)
• Ownership/funding structure & orientation tag (Left/C‑L/C/ C‑R/Right, etc.) with citation.

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “The by‑line says ‘Opinion | The Washington Post’ dated 2025‑03‑18 … ownership under Nash Holdings, generally Center‑Left per Media Bias Fact Check.”
Qs.Author Background & Declared Viewpoint:
Summarize potential bias or expertise signals **external to the text**:
• Author’s profession, past beats, public political affiliations, prior op‑eds.
• Notable ideological statements or controversies.
• Outlet editorial stance or endorsement history.
If nothing substantial is found, write: "No additional background located."

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Columnist is senior fellow at AEI, regularly argues for free‑market reforms; book ‘End the Tax Burden’ (2022) indicates fiscal‑conservative lean…”
Qs.Thesis Statement:
Paraphrase in ≤ 40 words the core argument or position the author advances. Do **not** inject analysis here—just capture the claim clearly.

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Author argues that federal student‑loan forgiveness unfairly shifts costs onto blue‑collar taxpayers and should be overturned by Congress.”
Qs.Supporting Evidence List:
Create a markdown table capturing every distinct piece of support the author offers.
| # | Evidence (quote or paraphrase) | Evidence Type (Fact / Statistic / Anecdote / Expert Quote / Analogy) | Fact vs. Opinion Flag | Source Cited? (Y/N) |
Include even rhetorical flourishes framed as evidence.

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Cites ‘analysts say plan costs $400 B’ → Statistic, Fact, Source Y (refers to CBO).”
Qs.Counterarguments:
Does the article acknowledge opposing views? Provide a short paragraph:
• Counter‑viewpoint(s) summarized.
• How the author addresses or dismisses them (rebuttal strength, tone, evidence used).
If no counterargument appears, state that explicitly.

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Author briefly notes critics who see forgiveness as stimulus; dismisses as ‘naïve’ without data ⇒ weak engagement.”
Qs.Persuasive Techniques:
Bullet list textual or structural devices used to persuade. Possible facets:
• Emotional language / moral framing
• Use of anecdotes or personal stories
• Logical fallacies (straw man, slippery slope, ad hominem, etc.)
• Repetition, rhetorical questions, hyperbole, sarcasm

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Invokes ‘hard‑working plumbers’ anecdote, uses phrase ‘crushing burden’ → emotional appeal; labels opponents ‘elitists’ → ad hominem.”
Qs.Fact‑Check of Empirical Claims:
Identify empirical (checkable) claims within the op‑ed and verify using IR snippets. Produce a markdown table:
| # | Claim (paraphrase) | External Source Used | Verification Result (True / False / Mixed / Unverifiable) | Notes |
If the op‑ed presents **no** checkable facts, state that plainly.

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Claim: ‘Plan costs $400 B’ → CBO estimate confirms range → True. Claim: ‘70 % of benefits go to top quintile’ → Brookings shows figure is ~30 % → False.”
